macro_segments.title: The Common Denominator (Hatzad Hashaveh) and Its Function in Categorizing Damages

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Common Denominator Principle and Its Role in Organizing the Four Categories
The Question of Whether Mammon (Ownership) is Required and Whether Shmiratan Alecha (Obligation to Watch) is a Criterion or a Consequence
Textual Variants and Textual Criticism: Whether Mammoncha Appears in the Mishna
The Abstract Principle of the Common Denominator and Its Application to Cases That Don't Fit Neatly Into Single Categories

macro_segments.title: Abaye's First Example: Stone, Knife, and Bundle Falling from a Roof in the Wind

micro_segments.title: Setting Up the Case: Objects Left on a Roof That Fall in the Wind and Cause Damage
Distinguishing Between Damage During the Fall (Which Would Be Eish) and Damage After Landing
The Characteristic of Eish: Another Force Mixed In (Koach Acher Meurav Bo)
Comparison to Eish and Bor: Learning From Both Categories to Characterize This Case

macro_segments.title: Case of Renouncing Ownership (Afkerinu): When Objects Fall and Become a Bor in Reshus Harabim

micro_segments.title: Scenario Where Objects Become Hefker and Constitute a Bor in Reshus Harabim
The Definition of Bor: Starting Life as a Hazard (Techilat Asiyaso Lenezek)
Tosafot's Intervention: Removing the Line About Rav's View That This Is a Shor, Not a Simple Bor
Tosafot's Nuanced View: Characterizing Property in Reshus Harabim as a Hybrid of Shor and Bor
The Logic for Including Such Cases Under Hatzad Hashaveh: Comparing to Eish and Bor

macro_segments.title: Rava's First Addition: A Bor That Is Moved Around by People and Animals (Bor Hamisgalgel)

micro_segments.title: The Case: A Bor or Knapsack Kicked Around by Feet of People and Animals
Analysis When Objects Are Renounced: Both Rav and Shmuel Agree It Remains a Bor
Why This Requires the Common Denominator: Koach Acher (Other Force) of People and Animals
Comparison to Shor and Bor: Learning That Even Movement by Others Doesn't Exclude It

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's Extended Discussion on Multiple Liable Parties: When Both Adam Hamazik and Mamon Hamazik Apply

micro_segments.title: Textual Issue: Whether a Line Distinguishing Adam Hamazik from Eish Is Included in Manuscripts
The Principle: When a Conscious Agent (Bar Deia) Is Involved, It Becomes Entirely That Agent's Action
Example of Distinction: A Dog (Not Bar Deia) Versus a Human (Bar Deia) Carrying a Fire
Tosafot's Conclusion: Adam Hamazik Eclipses Mamon Hamazik When Negligence Is Involved

macro_segments.title: Rav Acha bar Abba's Example: Draining Refuse Water into Reshus Harabim During Summer Months

micro_segments.title: Setting: People Who Open Drainpipes and Sweep Refuse into Public Domain Only When Permitted in Summer
Distinguishing Damage During Active Pouring from Damage After Water Settles
The Key Insight: Permission to Do an Act Does Not Automatically Grant Exemption from Damage Caused by Negligence
Comparison to Shor and Bor: Learning That Liability Persists Even With Permission

macro_segments.title: Ravina's Example: A Wall or Tree That Falls in Reshus Harabim After Warning or Without Warning

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Rule: Exemption When a Wall or Tree Falls Without Warning; Liability After Warning Period Expires
The Case Analyzed: Objects Renounced and Landed in Reshus Harabim
The Critical Distinction: Bor's Techilat Asiyaso Lenezek versus These Objects' Later Emergence as Hazards
Learning From Shor: That Objects Need Not Start Life as Hazards to Incur Liability
Tosafot's Note on Damage During the Fall: Whether It Constitutes Eish or Moving Bor

macro_segments.title: Transition to Liability Rules and Payment Obligation: Chav Hamazik and Tashlumei Nezek

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Statement: When Damage Occurs, the Damager Is Obligated to Pay
Terminology Question: Chav versus Chayav and Linguistic Variants of the Mishna
Introduction to the Payment Standard: Meitav Sadeihu—Paying from the Best of One's Land

macro_segments.title: The Debate Between Rabbi Yishmael and Rabbi Akiva on the Meaning of 'Best of the Land' (Meitav Sadeihu)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yishmael's View: Collection Based on the Best Quality of the Injured Party's Land
Rabbi Akiva's View: Collection from the Best Quality of the Damager's Own Land
Initial Interpretation: Why Rabbi Yishmael Might Be Seen as Paying for the Best Eaten Crop
Rav Idi bar Avin's Clarification: The Case of Uncertainty When the Exact Quality Eaten Cannot Be Determined
Rava's Objection: The Burden of Proof Should Remain With the Claimant (Hamotzi Mechavero)

macro_segments.title: Rav Acha bar Yaakov's Resolution: Different Standards Based on Relative Quality of Parties' Land

micro_segments.title: The Case Framework: When Shimon's Best Land Quality Equals Reuven's Worst Land Quality
Rabbi Yishmael's Position: Using Gezeira Shava to Link Both Passages to the Injured Party's Land
Rabbi Akiva's Position: The Verse Speaks of the Damager's Best Land Without Reference to the Injured Party
The Synthesizing Principle: When Reuven Has Land of Quality Equal to Shimon's Best, Collect That Quality; When Not, Collect from Reuven's Best
